• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Islander Drug Analysis

Kristina Dmytruk

Islander Dataset Analysis

This analysis explores the effect of different experimental drugs on memory improvement in the Islander dataset. The study evaluates overall memory improvement, compares drug treatments, and investigates potential influences such as dosage, age group, family background, and state of mood.

Research Questions

This analysis aims to answer the following questions:

  1. Do participants show overall memory improvement after treatment?
  2. Do different drugs produce different levels of improvement?
  3. Does drug dosage influence memory improvement?
  4. Does age affect treatment response?
  5. Does emotional valence influence recall?
  6. Do individuals from the same family respond similarly to treatment?

Load Required Libraries

library(tidyverse)
library(readr)
library(reshape2)
library(ggpubr)
library(knitr)

Load dataset

Islander_data <- read_csv(
  "~/Documents/R programming/Elixir course/2025-04-23-R-basic-main/2025-04-23-R-basic-main/data/Islander_data.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

Data exploration

Inspecting dataset structure and checking for missing values

# Print first 10 rows
head(Islander_data, 10)

# Descriptive statistics
summary(Islander_data)
  first_name         last_name              age        Happy_Sad_group   
 Length:198         Length:198         Min.   :24.00   Length:198        
 Class :character   Class :character   1st Qu.:30.00   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Median :37.00   Mode  :character  
                                       Mean   :39.53                     
                                       3rd Qu.:48.00                     
                                       Max.   :83.00                     
     Dosage         Drug           Mem_Score_Before Mem_Score_After 
 Min.   :1.00   Length:198         Min.   : 27.20   Min.   : 27.10  
 1st Qu.:1.00   Class :character   1st Qu.: 46.52   1st Qu.: 47.17  
 Median :2.00   Mode  :character   Median : 54.80   Median : 56.75  
 Mean   :1.99                      Mean   : 57.97   Mean   : 60.92  
 3rd Qu.:3.00                      3rd Qu.: 68.40   3rd Qu.: 73.25  
 Max.   :3.00                      Max.   :110.00   Max.   :120.00  
      Diff        
 Min.   :-40.400  
 1st Qu.: -3.175  
 Median :  1.700  
 Mean   :  2.955  
 3rd Qu.:  5.925  
 Max.   : 49.000  
# Check missing values
colSums(is.na(Islander_data))
      first_name        last_name              age  Happy_Sad_group 
               0                0                0                0 
          Dosage             Drug Mem_Score_Before  Mem_Score_After 
               0                0                0                0 
            Diff 
               0

Memory Scores Before and After Treatment

Calculation of the overall average memory scores before and after drug administration

average_memory_score_before <- mean(Islander_data$Mem_Score_Before)
paste('Average before is', average_memory_score_before)
[1] "Average before is 57.9676767676768"
average_memory_score_after <- mean(Islander_data$Mem_Score_After)
paste('Average after is', average_memory_score_after)
[1] "Average after is 60.9222222222222"
t_test_memory_improvment <- t.test(
  Islander_data$Mem_Score_After,
  Islander_data$Mem_Score_Before,
  paired = TRUE
)
t_test_memory_improvment

    Paired t-test

data:  Islander_data$Mem_Score_After and Islander_data$Mem_Score_Before
t = 3.8657, df = 197, p-value = 0.0001504
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 1.447293 4.461798
sample estimates:
mean difference 
       2.954545

Data exploration conclusion

According to paired t-test we can conclude that there is a significant difference in the memory score after drug administration.

Within-drug effect

Memory improvement summary by Drug

Islander_data %>% 
  group_by(Drug) %>%  #part 1
  summarize(
    Mean = mean(Diff, na.rm = TRUE), #the average diff in memory scores
    Median = median(Diff, na.rm = TRUE), #median 
    Standard_deviation = sd(Diff, na.rm = TRUE) #variance - standard deviation 
)

Introducing color-coding for each drug

drug_colors <- c("A" = "red", "S" = "green", "T" = "blue")

Memory improvement comparison visualization

summary_plot <- Islander_data %>%
  group_by(Drug) %>%
  summarise(mean_diff = mean(Diff, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Drug, y = mean_diff, fill = Drug)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, alpha=0.5) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Memory Improvement by Drug",
    x = "Drug",
    y = "Average Memory Score Improvement"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = drug_colors)

summary_plot

ggsave(
  filename = "Memory_improvement_summary.pdf",
  plot = summary_plot,
  width = 8,
  height = 5
)

Paired t-tests for Memory Improvement by Drug

To determine whether memory scores significantly improved after treatment, paired t-tests are performed for each drug separately.


t_test_memory_improvment_A <- t.test(
  Islander_data$Mem_Score_After[Islander_data$Drug == "A"],
  Islander_data$Mem_Score_Before[Islander_data$Drug == "A"],
  paired = TRUE
)
t_test_memory_improvment_A

    Paired t-test

data:  Islander_data$Mem_Score_After[Islander_data$Drug == "A"] and Islander_data$Mem_Score_Before[Islander_data$Drug == "A"]
t = 5.8486, df = 66, p-value = 1.69e-07
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
  6.237262 12.703037
sample estimates:
mean difference 
       9.470149 
t_test_memory_improvment_S <- t.test(
  Islander_data$Mem_Score_After[Islander_data$Drug == "S"],
  Islander_data$Mem_Score_Before[Islander_data$Drug == "S"],
  paired = TRUE
)
t_test_memory_improvment_S

    Paired t-test

data:  Islander_data$Mem_Score_After[Islander_data$Drug == "S"] and Islander_data$Mem_Score_Before[Islander_data$Drug == "S"]
t = -0.18546, df = 65, p-value = 0.8534
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -2.014923  1.672499
sample estimates:
mean difference 
     -0.1712121 
t_test_memory_improvment_T <- t.test(
  Islander_data$Mem_Score_After[Islander_data$Drug == "T"],
  Islander_data$Mem_Score_Before[Islander_data$Drug == "T"],
  paired = TRUE
)
t_test_memory_improvment_T

    Paired t-test

data:  Islander_data$Mem_Score_After[Islander_data$Drug == "T"] and Islander_data$Mem_Score_Before[Islander_data$Drug == "T"]
t = -0.66692, df = 64, p-value = 0.5072
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -2.348106  1.172722
sample estimates:
mean difference 
     -0.5876923

Within-drug effect conclusion

Paired t-tests indicate that Drug A produces a statistically significant improvement in memory scores relative to baseline.
Drugs S and T do not show statistically significant improvements when analyzed separately.

Between-drug comparison

model <- lm(Diff ~ Drug, data = Islander_data)
anova(model)
Analysis of Variance Table

Response: Diff
           Df  Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
Drug        2  4304.8 2152.39  22.711 1.359e-09 ***
Residuals 195 18480.5   94.77                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1
tukey_results <- TukeyHSD(aov(model))
tukey_results
  Tukey multiple comparisons of means
    95% family-wise confidence level

Fit: aov(formula = model)

$Drug
           diff       lwr       upr     p adj
S-A  -9.6413614 -13.62876 -5.653958 0.0000001
T-A -10.0578416 -14.06067 -6.055017 0.0000000
T-S  -0.4164802  -4.43421  3.601249 0.9675045

Visual exploration: box plots

Memory_improvment_by_drug <- ggplot(Islander_data, aes(x = Drug, y = Diff, fill = Drug)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.4) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Memory Improvement by Drug",
    y = "Memory Score Difference"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = drug_colors)

Memory_improvment_by_drug

ggsave(
  filename = "Memory_Improvment_by_Drug.pdf",
  plot = Memory_improvment_by_drug,
  width = 8,
  height = 5
)

Between-drug conclusion

The linear model and ANOVA indicate a significant effect of drug treatment on memory improvement (F(2,195) = 22.71, p < 0.001).

Post-hoc Tukey comparisons show:

  • Drug A improves memory significantly better than Drug S (p < 0.001) and Drug T (p < 0.001).
  • No significant difference is observed between Drugs S and T (p = 0.968).

Thus, Drug A demonstrates the strongest and most consistent improvement, whereas S and T have similar, weaker effects.
Further analyses will primarily focus on Drug A.

Dosage-response analysis

The dose–response shows how memory improvement changes with increasing drug dosage.

If improvement increases with dosage, this suggests a potential dose-dependent treatment effect.

dosage_summary <- Islander_data %>%
  group_by(Drug, Dosage) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_diff = mean(Diff, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Drug), mean_diff) %>%
  mutate(Dosage = factor(Dosage))  # convert Dosage to factor

Visualization: Dose–Response Relationship

dosage_effect <- ggplot(dosage_summary, aes(x = Dosage, y = mean_diff, color = Drug, group = Drug)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~Drug) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Dose–Response Relationship by Drug",
    x = "Dosage",
    y = "Average Memory Improvement"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = drug_colors)
  
dosage_effect 

#Save the plot as a PDF file
ggsave(
  filename = "Dosage_effect.pdf",
  plot = dosage_effect,
  width = 8,
  height = 5
)

Age_group analysis

#Add new column age_group
Islander_data <- Islander_data %>%
  mutate(
    Age_group = case_when(
      age < 30 ~ 'Young',
      age > 50 ~ 'Senior',
      TRUE ~ 'Middle age'
    ),
    Age_group = factor(Age_group, levels = c("Young", "Middle age", "Senior"))
  ) 
  
#Group by Age_group and Drug  
Islander_data %>%
  group_by(Age_group, Drug) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_diff = mean(Diff, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) 
NA

Statistics: does age group affects response to Drug A

To determine whether the effectiveness of Drug A varies across age groups, a linear model was fitted with memory improvement as the dependent variable and age group as the predictor.

The ANOVA test evaluates whether the average improvement differs between age groups.

age_effect_model <- Islander_data %>%
  filter(Drug == "A") %>%
  lm(Diff ~ Age_group, data = .)

summary(age_effect_model)

Call:
lm(formula = Diff ~ Age_group, data = .)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-23.556  -8.158  -0.487   7.590  36.240 

Coefficients:
                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)  
(Intercept)            4.488      3.238   1.386   0.1707  
Age_groupMiddle age    8.272      3.909   2.116   0.0382 *
Age_groupSenior        2.769      4.580   0.605   0.5476  
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Residual standard error: 12.95 on 64 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.0737,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.04475 
F-statistic: 2.546 on 2 and 64 DF,  p-value: 0.08631
anova(age_effect_model)
Analysis of Variance Table

Response: Diff
          Df  Sum Sq Mean Sq F value  Pr(>F)  
Age_group  2   854.5  427.23   2.546 0.08631 .
Residuals 64 10739.5  167.81                  
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Age group effect conclusion

There is no statistically significant effect of age group on the efficacy of Drug A at the 0.05 significance level.

Visualization: Strength of Drug A effect on different age groups

#Visualization: Drug_A_effect_across_age_group 
Drug_A_effect_accross_age_group <- Islander_data %>%
  filter(Drug == "A") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Age_group, y = Diff, fill = Age_group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 1) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Drug A Effect Across Age Groups",
    x = "Age Group",
    y = "Memory Score Improvement"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Young" = "#fcbba1",
    "Middle age" = "#fb6a4a",
    "Senior" = "#cb181d"
  ))

Drug_A_effect_accross_age_group

#Save the plot as a PDF file
ggsave(
  filename = "Drug_A_effect_across_age_group.pdf",
  plot = Drug_A_effect_accross_age_group,
  width = 8,
  height = 5
)

Memories emotional valence analysis

emotion_summary <- Islander_data %>%
  group_by(Happy_Sad_group) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_diff = mean(Diff, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

#Visualization:
emotion_effect <- ggplot(emotion_summary, aes(x = Happy_Sad_group, y = mean_diff, fill = Happy_Sad_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(S = "lightblue", H = "lightcoral")) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Memory Improvement by emotional valence",
    x = "Emotional valence",
    y = "Average Memory Improvement"
  )
emotion_effect

#Save the plot as a PDF file
ggsave(
  filename = "Drug_effect_emotional_valence.pdf",
  plot = emotion_effect,
  width = 8,
  height = 5
)

Statistics: does emotional valence influence recall after drugs administration?

mood_t_test <- t.test(
  Diff ~ Happy_Sad_group,
  data = Islander_data
)

mood_t_test

    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  Diff by Happy_Sad_group
t = -0.28876, df = 194.76, p-value = 0.7731
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group H and group S is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -3.464183  2.579334
sample estimates:
mean in group H mean in group S 
       2.733333        3.175758

Emotional valence conclusion:

No significant difference observed in recall improvement between happy and sad memories.

However, there could be difference in recall improvement between happy and sad memories for certain drug.

Genetical background: response to treatment by family

#Add column Family
Islander_data <- Islander_data %>%
  mutate(Family = factor(last_name))

#Heatmap: Response to treatment by family and drug
Islander_data_filtered <- Islander_data %>%
  group_by(Family) %>%
  filter(n_distinct(Drug) == 3) %>%  # Assuming 3 unique treatments
  ungroup() 

#Reshape data without aggregation
data_matrix <- dcast(Islander_data_filtered, Family ~ Drug, value.var = "Diff", fun.aggregate = mean)  # using mean or any other aggregation if needed

#Melt the data to long format for plotting
data_long <- melt(data_matrix, id.vars = "Family", variable.name = "Drug", value.name = "Diff")

#Plot the heatmap
Heatmap_TreatmentResponse_ByFamile <- ggplot(data_long, aes(x = Drug, y = Family, fill = Diff)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "lightblue", high = "lightcoral") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Drug", y = "Family", title = "Heatmap of Treatment Response by Family") 

Heatmap_TreatmentResponse_ByFamile

#Save the plot as a PDF file
ggsave(
  filename = "Heatmap_TreatmentResponse_ByFamile.pdf",
  plot = Heatmap_TreatmentResponse_ByFamile,
  width = 8,
  height = 5
)

Genetical background conclusion

Members of different families were treated with one of the experimental drugs. The heatmap indicates that noticeable memory improvement is primarily observed among participants who received Drug A. This observation is consistent with the earlier statistical analysis, which showed that only Drug A produces a significant improvement in memory scores relative to baseline.

Overall, these results suggest that the positive effect of Drug A on memory performance does not appear to depend strongly on family background, implying that genetic differences between families are unlikely to be a major driver of the observed improvement. At the same time, the heatmap shows that some families did not experience noticeable improvement under any treatment, indicating that individual or family-level factors may still influence responsiveness to the drugs.

Final Conclusion

The present analysis investigated the effect of experimental drugs on memory improvement using the Islander dataset. Several statistical approaches were applied to evaluate treatment effects and explore potential factors influencing drug response.

A paired t-test comparing memory scores before and after treatment demonstrated a statistically significant improvement in memory performance following drug administration (t = 3.87, p < 0.001), indicating that treatment overall was associated with enhanced recall ability.

To determine whether different drugs produced different effects, a linear model followed by ANOVA and Tukey post-hoc tests was conducted. These analyses revealed a significant effect of drug type on memory improvement (F(2,195) = 22.71, p < 0.001). Post-hoc comparisons showed that Drug A resulted in significantly greater improvement than both Drug S and Drug T, while no significant difference was observed between Drugs S and T.

Further analyses investigated potential moderating factors. Dose–response patterns suggested that higher dosages may be associated with increased memory improvement, particularly for Drug A. Age group analysis indicated modest differences in response, with middle-aged participants showing slightly higher improvements compared to younger participants, although overall age effects were limited.

Finally, examination of family-level patterns using a heatmap suggested that the beneficial effect of Drug A was broadly consistent across families. While some families did not show strong responses to any treatment, the overall pattern indicates that genetic background is unlikely to be the primary driver of the observed treatment effect.

Taken together, the results indicate that Drug A demonstrates the most promising effect on memory improvement among the tested treatments. These findings highlight the importance of drug selection in cognitive enhancement studies and suggest that Drug A warrants further investigation in controlled experimental settings.
